common_name: Orange Jessamine (Mock Orange, Chalcas, China Box)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after last frost; in zone 9 plant February–April for the longest establishment period before summer heat. Orange Jessamine tolerates light freezes (28–32°F) but will be damaged by temperatures below 28°F — in zone 9a plant in a sheltered south-facing location or near a heat-retaining wall for best winter survival. It blooms multiple times per year in zone 9-10 when temperatures are warm.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Orange Jessamine is one of the most intensely fragrant shrubs available for zone 9 Gulf Coast and Florida gardens, producing multiple flushes of tiny, pure-white, star-shaped flowers throughout the warm months — the fragrance has been described as a combination of orange blossom and jasmine and is one of the strongest and most intoxicating scents in the subtropical plant world. Murraya paniculata is closely related to citrus (family Rutaceae) and is native from South Asia through southern China and into Malaysia; it has been naturalized in South Florida where it occasionally escapes into natural areas. The glossy, dark-green, aromatic foliage is attractive year-round; after the white flowers fade, small oval berries ripen from green to orange-red, adding additional ornamental interest. In zone 9, Orange Jessamine is an excellent fragrant hedge, privacy screen, or specimen shrub reaching 8–15 feet; it can also be maintained as a small tree with lower branch removal. It tolerates light shade well and performs admirably as a foundation shrub under building overhangs or in bright indirect light. Prune immediately after flowering to control size; the plant flowers on new wood. NOTE: Murraya paniculata is a host plant for citrus greening disease (Huanglongbing) and its import is restricted in some states — check local regulations before purchasing.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Murraya paniculata (Orange Jessamine)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Murraya paniculata
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Fragrant Shrubs for the Gulf South
USDA APHIS — Murraya paniculata as Huanglongbing host plant

common_name: Texas Sabal Palm (Rio Grande Sabal, Texas Palmetto)

planting.note: Plant container-grown palms in spring when soil is warm; Texas Sabal Palm is very slow-growing but extremely long-lived. It can handle brief dips to 15–18°F when established, making it the most cold-hardy native palm in Texas. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil; avoid persistently waterlogged conditions. Newly transplanted palms should be tied to prevent wind rock for the first two growing seasons.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil of any type; Texas Sabal Palm is drought-tolerant once established and adapts to alkaline, sandy, or clay soils throughout zone 8–11. Sabal mexicana (also listed as Sabal texana in older references) is the native fan palm of the Lower Rio Grande Valley of Texas and northeastern Mexico, where it once formed extensive palm forests along the Rio Grande floodplain — nearly all of which were cleared for agriculture, leaving only a few hundred wild specimens at Sabal Palm Sanctuary near Brownsville, Texas (one of the most endangered plant communities in the continental United States). The imposing, single-trunk palm develops a shaggy, fibrous trunk and large crown of blue-green fan fronds that bend gracefully in wind; mature specimens in cultivation reach 50 feet. The small, round, dark purple fruits (produced on female palms — the species is dioecious) are eaten by birds and were used by indigenous peoples as food. Texas Sabal Palm is among the cold-hardiest palms in North America, proven to survive temperatures to 15°F when established, making it the most reliable large single-trunk palm for zone 8b Gulf Coast gardens. Plant it as a legacy tree — specimens planted today will be magnificent landscape specimens in 50 years.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Sabal mexicana (Texas Sabal Palm)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Sabal mexicana
The Nature Conservancy — Sabal Palm Sanctuary, Brownsville Texas
University of Texas at Austin Biodiversity Center — Sabal mexicana

common_name: Evergreen Blueberry (Darrow's Blueberry, Lowbush Evergreen Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, sandy or amended soil; Darrow's Blueberry requires the same acidic conditions as other blueberries. Plant 2–3 varieties for cross-pollination. In zone 9, it requires only 100–200 chill hours, making it one of the few blueberries that reliably flowers and fruits even in warm Gulf Coast winters.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic, sandy or amended soil in full sun to partial shade; in hot zone 9 climates, afternoon shade improves fruit quality. Vaccinium darrowii (Darrow's Blueberry or Evergreen Blueberry) is a native, low-growing blueberry species from the coastal plain flatwoods and sandhills of the Southeast, ranging from South Carolina through Florida to Louisiana. It is unique among native blueberries for being semi-evergreen to fully evergreen — retaining most of its small, bluish-gray leaves through mild winters — and for requiring very few chill hours (100–200 hours below 45°F), making it one of the most reliable blueberries for warm zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens where the commonly-planted Rabbiteye types (Vaccinium ashei) may not receive enough cold in mild years. Vaccinium darrowii is also an important breeding parent used by blueberry breeders at the University of Florida and USDA to develop the Southern Highbush blueberry series — its drought tolerance, evergreen foliage, and minimal chill requirement were bred into commercial cultivars. The wild species produces small but sweet, intensely flavored berries that are beloved by wildlife. It tolerates the high heat, humidity, and wet-dry cycles of Gulf Coast flatwoods better than most northern blueberry species.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Vaccinium darrowii (Darrow's Blueberry)
USDA Plants Database — Vaccinium darrowii

common_name: Swamp Jessamine (Rankin's Jessamine, Scentless Yellow Jessamine)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring in moist to wet, partially shaded sites; Swamp Jessamine naturally grows in floodplain forests, swampy margins, and moist woodland edges. Unlike Carolina Jessamine (G. sempervirens) which thrives in dry, sunny sites, Swamp Jessamine needs consistently moist soil and partial shade. All parts of the plant are toxic — like all Gelsemium species, do not confuse with edible plants.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, acidic soil along a fence, trellis, or arbor; Swamp Jessamine twines to 10–20 feet and can cover a large support structure. Gelsemium rankinii is a native evergreen vine closely related to Carolina Jessamine (G. sempervirens) but adapted to entirely different habitat — wet, shady floodplains and swamp margins rather than dry, sunny slopes. The yellow, tubular, slightly fragrant flowers (though less intensely fragrant than G. sempervirens) appear in two distinct blooming periods: late winter–spring (like Carolina Jessamine) AND again in fall (October–December), making it a unique vine that provides yellow blooms in both early spring and late fall when few other plants are in color. The glossy, dark-green evergreen foliage is attractive year-round. Swamp Jessamine fills a specific ecological niche as a shade-tolerant, moisture-loving native vine for moist woodland settings in zone 6–9 where Carolina Jessamine would fail in the shade and wet conditions. CAUTION: Like all Gelsemium species, all parts of the plant are highly toxic — containing gelsemine and other alkaloids that can be fatal. Do not confuse with edible plants. Plant away from children and pets.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Gelsemium rankinii (Swamp Jessamine)
USDA Plants Database — Gelsemium rankinii

common_name: Slender Lady Palm (Small Rhapis, Miniature Lady Palm)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in partial to deep shade; Slender Lady Palm is one of the finest palms for shaded garden situations and container culture. It accepts less light than almost any other palm. Protect from temperatures below 25–28°F in zone 9a; established clumps in zone 9b often survive brief cold snaps by resprouting from the base. Excellent as a large container specimen that can be moved indoors during cold snaps.

directions: Plant in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; unlike most palms, Slender Lady Palm thrives and indeed prefers shaded conditions — it will sunburn in direct afternoon sun in zone 9. Rhapis humilis is a clustering, multi-stem palm from southern China closely related to the Lady Palm (Rhapis excelsa) but more slender-stemmed, more shade-tolerant, and typically shorter (5–10 feet versus R. excelsa's potential 15 feet). The palm forms elegant, slowly expanding clumps of dark-green, glossy, fan-shaped fronds on slim, bamboo-like stems covered with persistent leaf sheaths. In zone 9 Gulf Coast landscapes, it is an outstanding shade garden palm — one of the few palms that can be planted in deep shade under large trees and will thrive in the low-light conditions that challenge most ornamentals. Slender Lady Palm is also a classic choice for large container culture: slow growth, elegant form, tolerance of indoor conditions, and tidy habit make it a premier container palm for patios, entryways, and atriums. In zone 9a, established clumps in protected locations often resprout from the base if tops are frozen. Hardy to about 25°F for brief periods with established roots.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rhapis humilis (Slender Lady Palm)
